paper_title: Paleoclimate Reconstruction using Foraminifera Isotopes and Trace Elements

methods.description: Procedure for determining stable oxygen (δ¹⁸O) and carbon (δ¹³C) isotope compositions in handpicked planktonic foraminifera (G. ruber) using Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometry (IRMS) coupled with an automated carbonate preparation device.

steps.description: Oxygen and carbon stable isotopes were determined in the handpicked planktonic foraminifera G. ruber from the 250 to 355 μm size.
steps.searchable_summary: Select and size planktonic foraminifera samples.
outputs: Sized and selected foraminifera (G. ruber, 250-355 μm)

steps.description: Analyzed subsamples had masses of ~85 -105 μg, but as little as ~60 μg.
Samples were acidified under vacuum with 100% ortho-phosphoric acid, the resulting CO 2 was cryogenically purified, and delivered to the mass spectrometer.
steps.searchable_summary: Acidify samples, purify CO2, and prepare for IRMS analysis using automated system.
outputs: Purified CO2 gas ready for IRMS

steps.description: Stable-isotope compositions were determined using a Finnigan Delta IV Plus light stable-isotope ratio mass spectrometer (SIRMS).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure stable isotope ratios (δ¹⁸O, δ¹³C) using IRMS.
outputs: δ¹⁸O and δ¹³C values for each sample

steps.description: Approximately 10% of all samples were run in duplicate and showed reproducible results.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform duplicate analysis on a subset of samples to check reproducibility.

steps.description: The standard deviation of repeated measurements of an internal standard was ±0.023‰ for δ 13 C and ±0.045‰ for δ 18 O.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure internal standard repeatedly to assess analytical precision.
outputs: Standard deviation of internal standard measurements

steps.description: The overall analytical reproducibility, as determined from replicate measurements on the standard material (NBS-19), is routinely better than ±0.05‰ (±1 σ) for both δ 18 O and δ 13 C.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure external standard material (NBS-19) replicates to assess overall analytical reproducibility.
outputs: Overall analytical reproducibility (standard deviation) for external standard

methods.method_name: Foraminiferal Cleaning, Elemental Analysis (Mg/Ca), and Paleotemperature Estimation
methods.description: Procedure involving cleaning of planktonic foraminifera (G. ruber) to remove contaminants, analysis of minor and trace element concentrations (Mg, Ca, Sr, Al, Fe, Mn) using Inductively Coupled Plasma-Optical Emission Spectrometry (ICP-OES), and subsequent estimation of Sea Surface Temperature (SST) and seawater oxygen isotope composition (δ¹⁸Osw) based on Mg/Ca ratios and other corrections.

steps.description: Approximately twenty G. ruber tests from the 250 to 355 μm size fraction were gently crushed under a binocular microscope, but care was taken not to pulverize broken shell fragments.
steps.searchable_summary: Select and gently crush foraminifera tests from a specific size fraction.

steps.description: Crushed samples were cleaned according to the Boyle and Keigwin (1985/86) cleaning protocol, but changing the order to mechanical (multiple sonication before treating samples by trace metal grade methanol), then reductive [using hydrazine in citric acid and NH 4 (OH) buffer], and oxidative cleaning (using NaOH and H 2 O 2 ) and final polishing with weak (0.001 M) nitric acid.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform multi-step chemical cleaning procedure on crushed foraminifera samples.

steps.description: Samples were dissolved by 0.2 normal nitric acid and a 300 μl of aliquot was removed and diluted by 0.2 normal nitric acid to make up to ~1 ml solution for analyses.
steps.searchable_summary: Dissolve cleaned samples and prepare diluted aliquots for elemental analysis.
outputs: Diluted sample solutions (~1 ml) for ICP-OES

steps.description: Aliquots were analyzed on a Perkin Elmer 4300 dual view inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) at the College of Marine Science, University of South Florida. Mg, Ca, and Sr were the target elements for quantitative purpose, whereas the Al, Fe, and Mn were routinely measured simultaneously to evaluate the cleaning protocol.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure elemental concentrations (Mg, Ca, Sr, Al, Fe, Mn) in sample solutions using ICP-OES.
outputs: Concentrations of Mg, Ca, Sr, Al, Fe, Mn for each sample

steps.description: Results indicate an analytical precision for Mg/Ca of ≤ 0.4% root-mean standard deviation (1 σ). The average precision of all data based on duplicates or triplicates is ±0.15 for Mg/Ca, which corresponds to ±0.4°C.
steps.searchable_summary: Assess analytical precision of Mg/Ca measurements using replicates.
inputs: Mg/Ca measurements from samples and replicates
outputs: Analytical precision metrics (RSD, standard deviation) for Mg/Ca

steps.description: SST was estimated from Mg/Ca ratios using a species-specific equation developed from North Atlantic sediment-trap and core-top calibrations: Mg/Ca = 0.38xe (0.09xSST) , where the Mg/Ca concentration ratio in the foraminiferal test is in mmol/mol and the SST is in °C (Elderfield and Ganssen 2000;Anand et al. 2003).
steps.searchable_summary: Estimate Sea Surface Temperature (SST) from Mg/Ca ratios using a specific calibration equation.

steps.description: We used G. ruber-specific calibration of Mulitza et al. (2003) [δ 18 O sw = (T Mg/Ca -14.2) / 4.44 + δ 18 O] to calculate δ 18 O sw.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate seawater oxygen isotope composition (δ¹⁸Osw) using estimated SST (T_Mg/Ca) and measured δ¹⁸O values with a specific calibration equation.

steps.description: converted them to Vienna Standard Mean Ocean Water (VSMOW) by adding 0.27‰ to the estimated δ 18 O sw values (Hut 1987).
steps.searchable_summary: Convert δ¹⁸Osw values to VSMOW scale.
inputs: Calculated δ¹⁸Osw values (relative to PDB or other scale)

steps.description: Effects of global ice volume change were corrected using a sea-level record from Fairbanks (1989) to separate residual δ 18 O sw for the Bay of Bengal. A relationship between the δ 18 O sw and sealevel of 0.0083‰ m -1 sea-level (Adkins et al. 2002) was also applied.
steps.searchable_summary: Correct δ¹⁸Osw values for global ice volume changes using a sea-level record and a specified relationship.

steps.description: We have used the foraminiferal weight (Rashid et al. 2007), instrumental Ca-signal, and any signature of shell mass loss under the microscope to assess dissolution (Saher et al. 2007). We have not encountered any dissolution event.
steps.searchable_summary: Assess sample dissolution using multiple indicators (weight, Ca signal, microscopy).
